label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is about a passage that talks about memory tricks. The user is asking which statement is true according to the passage. First, I need to look at the passage again. The example given is: "One morning last week, for example, I got up and found that I had left the front door unlocked all night, yet I clearly remember locking it carefully the night before." So, the writer found the door unlocked in the morning, but he remembers locking it the night before. That means he thought he locked it, but actually didn't. So his memory tricked him into thinking he did lock it when in reality he didn't. Looking at the options: A) One night the writer forgot to lock the front door. This would mean he didn't lock it and also didn't remember doing so. But the passage says he remembers locking it, but actually didn't. So this is not correct. B) One night the writer forgot having locked the front door. Wait, this is a bit confusing. If he forgot having locked it, that would mean he didn't remember that he locked it. But the passage says he clearly remembers locking it. So B is not correct either. Wait, maybe I'm misunderstanding B. Let me check again. "Forgot having locked" might mean he forgot that he had locked it. But in the passage, he remembers locking it. So B is not correct. C) The writer remembered to lock the door. Well, he did remember locking it, but the problem is that he didn't actually lock it. So technically, he remembered doing it, but it didn't happen. The statement says "remembered to lock" which is not the same as "remembered that he locked". The wording here is tricky. The original passage says "I clearly remember locking it carefully the night before." So he remembers the action. So C could be correct,
